guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — Dermatology: contact dermatitis and acute dermatitis management
Australian Resuscitation Council / ASCIA — recognition and escalation of acute allergic reactions
intentional_test_features: Red-herring framing: the source note and filename foreground a chronic, benign scalp condition (patterned hair loss), but the scoreable acute problem is a severe painful weeping scalp reaction to an unregulated topical product. The test is whether the system RESISTS anchoring on 'just hair loss' and recognises the acute chemical/allergic skin injury.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present clinician who inspected the scalp, measured vitals and supervised decontamination. The AI Doctor cannot examine the scalp, cannot grade the depth of a chemical injury and cannot measure vitals without a device — it is scored on recognising the injury, instructing patient-performed decontamination, screening for airway/anaphylaxis features, and arranging URGENT same-day in-person assessment.

Narrow telehealth-limit tier uplift to T3: the patient is systemically well with no airway features (so not T4/T5), but the depth of a chemical injury and early secondary infection cannot be excluded remotely — a genuine unexcludable serious mimic that would need urgent in-person management if present.
Over-triage-resistance boundary: 000/ED is the WRONG answer while there are no airway or systemic features — the case penalises both under-action (routine reassurance) and reflexive over-escalation.
Age and sex inferred (adult male) — flagged; no paediatric dosing issue.
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue), source note DST-005.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
